history: The client reports recurrent dreams during graduate school about being unprepared for a thesis defense, indicating longstanding anxiety about performance and preparedness. The client's father is described as having high expectations, which likely contributed to internalized pressure to meet standards and fear of falling short. These patterns suggest a history of striving for approval and fearing criticism, with emotional distress linked to evaluative situations.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
intermediate_belief: If I am criticized or make a mistake, it means I am not good enough. I must always be fully prepared and meet expectations to avoid failure. Others will judge me harshly if I fall short.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I can't meet expectations or avoid criticism, there's no point in trying because I will always disappoint myself and others.
coping_strategies: The client tends to avoid situations involving evaluation or criticism and may withdraw or become defensive when criticized. They also seek reassurance or external validation to manage self-doubt and anxiety.
situation: Receiving criticism during a work performance review.
auto_thought: I'm not good enough; they're going to see that I can't meet their expectations.
behavior: I become quiet and withdrawn during the review. I avoid eye contact and try to minimize my responses. Later, I ruminate about what was said and seek reassurance from a colleague.
